title: The rice in the wedding pocket
summary: A girl asks her aunt about a small wedding custom — rice in the bride's pocket — and learns the quiet rules that go with it.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Auntie, why do we put rice in the bride's pocket at weddings?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: It is an old custom from the north. The rice is for the new family. It is small, but it has meaning.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Did you do it at your wedding?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: Yes. My mother put it there. I did not know until that day.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Was it strange?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: A little. The rice was hot. The pocket was small. I kept it for a year, in a jar.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: A whole year? Why?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: My grandmother told me to. She said, 'Eat one grain on each anniversary. When the jar is empty, you have been married long enough to keep it that way.'

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Did you eat them all?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: I ate twenty-eight. I had no more anniversaries with him.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Oh. I am sorry.

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: Do not be sorry, Lien. He was a good man. We had twenty-eight years.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Did you ever finish the jar another way?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: I gave the rest to my daughter. She put it in her own pocket on her wedding day.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: Has she eaten any?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: Two grains. She has only been married for two years.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: And when she finishes them?

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: She will put new rice in the jar. The old grains go. The new grains start. The jar stays.

body.speaker: Lien
body.text: That is beautiful.

body.speaker: Auntie Hong
body.text: It is also rice. Do not make it more than it is. But also do not make it less.

annotations.meaningVi: ngày kỷ niệm (đặc biệt là kỷ niệm ngày cưới)
giữ nguyên như vậy (cụm từ cố định — duy trì trạng thái hiện tại)
Đừng xin lỗi / Đừng buồn vì chuyện đó (cụm cố định)
đủ lâu (adverb + adjective — 'enough' đứng sau tính từ/trạng từ)
bảo tôi làm (cấu trúc: tell + object + to-infinitive — bảo ai làm gì)

annotations.phrase: do not make it more than it is
annotations.meaningVi: đừng thổi phồng ý nghĩa của nó (thành ngữ — đừng coi trọng quá mức)

grammarNotes.title: Talking about duration: 'for + period'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng 'for + khoảng thời gian' để nói một việc kéo dài bao lâu. Trong bài: 'I kept it for a year.' (Tôi đã giữ nó một năm.) / 'We had twenty-eight years.' / 'She has only been married for two years.' Có thể dùng với hiện tại hoàn thành (have/has been... for) hoặc quá khứ đơn (kept... for).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'for + period' to say how long something lasted. From the dialogue: 'I kept it for a year.' / 'We had twenty-eight years.' / 'She has only been married for two years.' Can be used with present perfect (has been… for) or past simple (kept… for).

cloze.template:
Lien: Auntie, why do we put {{b1}} in the bride's pocket at weddings?
Auntie Hong: It is an old custom from the {{b2}}. The rice is for the new family. It is small, but it has meaning.
Lien: Did you do it at your {{b3}}?
Auntie Hong: Yes. My mother put it there. I did not know until that day.
Lien: Was it strange?
Auntie Hong: A little. The rice was hot. The pocket was small. I kept it for a year, in a {{b4}}.
Lien: A whole year? Why?
Auntie Hong: My grandmother told me to. She said, 'Eat one {{b5}} on each {{b6}}. When the jar is empty, you have been married long enough to keep it that way.'
Lien: Did you eat them all?
Auntie Hong: I ate {{b7}}. I had no more anniversaries with him.
Lien: Oh. I am sorry.
Auntie Hong: Do not be sorry, Lien. He was a {{b8}} man. We had twenty-eight years.
Lien: Did you ever finish the jar another way?
Auntie Hong: I gave the rest to my {{b9}}. She put it in her own pocket on her wedding day.
Lien: Has she eaten any?
Auntie Hong: {{b10}} grains. She has only been married for two years.
Lien: And when she finishes them?
Auntie Hong: She will put new rice in the jar. The old {{b11}} go. The new grains start. The jar stays.
Lien: That is {{b12}}.
Auntie Hong: It is also rice. Do not make it more than it is. But also do not make it less.

blanks.explanation: Rice goes in the bride's pocket — the heart of the custom.
It is an old custom from the north.
Lien asks if Auntie did it at her own wedding.
She kept the rice in a jar for a year.
On each anniversary — the date of the wedding each year.
She ate twenty-eight grains — one for each anniversary.
'He was a good man. We had twenty-eight years.'
She gave the rest to her daughter, who put it in her own pocket on her wedding day.
The daughter has eaten two grains — she has been married for two years.
The old grains go; the new grains start. The jar stays.
Lien says, 'That is beautiful.' Auntie's reply pulls her back a little: it is also just rice.

translationVi:
Liên: Cô ơi, sao đám cưới mình lại bỏ gạo vào túi cô dâu vậy?
Cô Hồng: Đó là phong tục cũ ngoài bắc. Gạo là cho gia đình mới. Chuyện nhỏ, nhưng có ý nghĩa.
Liên: Đám cưới cô có làm vậy không?
Cô Hồng: Có. Mẹ cô bỏ gạo vào đó. Đến ngày cưới cô mới biết.
Liên: Cảm giác lạ không cô?
Cô Hồng: Cũng hơi. Gạo nóng. Túi bé. Cô đã giữ nó một năm, trong một cái hũ.
Liên: Cả một năm cơ ạ? Sao thế cô?
Cô Hồng: Bà cô bảo cô làm vậy. Bà nói: 'Mỗi ngày cưới ăn một hạt. Khi cái hũ rỗng, là cháu đã cưới đủ lâu để giữ nguyên cuộc hôn nhân.'
Liên: Cô ăn hết rồi à?
Cô Hồng: Cô ăn hai mươi tám hạt. Sau đó cô không còn dịp kỷ niệm nào với ông ấy nữa.
Liên: Ôi. Cháu xin lỗi.
Cô Hồng: Đừng xin lỗi, Liên. Ông ấy là người tốt. Bọn cô có hai mươi tám năm.
Liên: Cô có hết cái hũ theo cách nào khác không?
Cô Hồng: Phần còn lại cô cho con gái cô. Con bé bỏ vào túi mình hôm cưới.
Liên: Cô ấy đã ăn hạt nào chưa ạ?
Cô Hồng: Hai hạt. Con bé mới cưới hai năm thôi.
Liên: Rồi khi con gái cô ăn hết thì sao?
Cô Hồng: Nó sẽ bỏ gạo mới vào hũ. Hạt cũ đi. Hạt mới bắt đầu. Cái hũ ở lại.
Liên: Nghe đẹp quá cô.
Cô Hồng: Cũng là gạo thôi. Đừng làm cho nó lớn hơn nó vốn là. Nhưng cũng đừng làm cho nó nhỏ hơn.

questions.prompt: What does Lien ask about at the start?
options: Rice in the bride's pocket at weddings
questions.explanation: 'Why do we put rice in the bride's pocket at weddings?'

questions.prompt: Where does the custom come from?
questions.explanation: 'It is an old custom from the north.'

questions.prompt: Where did Auntie Hong keep the rice for a year?
questions.explanation: 'I kept it for a year, in a jar.'

questions.prompt: Why did her grandmother tell her to eat one grain a year?
options: Because empty jar = married long enough to keep the marriage
questions.explanation: 'When the jar is empty, you have been married long enough to keep it that way.'

questions.prompt: How many grains did Auntie Hong eat in total?
questions.explanation: 'I ate twenty-eight. I had no more anniversaries with him.'
questions.hint: Look at Auntie's reply about how many she ate.

questions.prompt: Why didn't she finish the jar?
options: Her husband passed away after twenty-eight years
Her grandmother told her to stop
questions.explanation: 'I had no more anniversaries with him.' — He had died, after twenty-eight years of marriage.
questions.hint: Look at the implication of 'no more anniversaries with him'.

questions.prompt: What did Auntie do with the rice that was left in the jar?
options: She gave it to her daughter, who put it in her own pocket at her wedding
questions.explanation: 'I gave the rest to my daughter. She put it in her own pocket on her wedding day.'
questions.hint: Look at Auntie's turn about her daughter.

questions.prompt: How long has the daughter been married?
questions.explanation: 'She has only been married for two years.'
questions.hint: Look at the answer about the daughter's grains.

questions.prompt: What will happen when the daughter eventually finishes the rice?
options: She will put new rice in the jar — the old grains go, the new grains start, but the jar stays
She will buy a new jar
She will return the jar to her mother
questions.explanation: 'The old grains go. The new grains start. The jar stays.'
questions.hint: Look at Auntie's turn about new rice.

questions.prompt: What does Auntie Hong say at the very end?
options: It is also rice — do not make it more than it is, but also not less
She wants to stop the tradition
questions.explanation: Her closing balances Lien's word 'beautiful' with the plain truth that the rice is also just rice.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Auntie Hong says, 'Do not make it more than it is. But also do not make it less.' Think of a small ritual in your family or your culture. What does it mean if you take it too seriously, and what does it mean if you treat it as nothing at all?
